title: interface vs type
summary: Both describe object shapes. Interfaces merge via declaration merging; type aliases are closed unions or intersections.
content:
## When to use interface

- Public APIs you may extend.
- When you want **declaration merging** (multiple declarations combine).

## When to use type

- Union and intersection types (`A | B`).
- Mapped types, conditional types, template literals.

For many teams, **pick one style for objects** and stay consistent.

related_questions.preview: interface vs type in production codebases

title: Generics
summary: Generics parameterize types so functions and data structures stay type-safe while working with many concrete types.
content:
## Basics

`function identity<T>(x: T): T { return x; }`

## Constraints

Use `extends` to require shape: `function keys<T extends object>(obj: T)`.

## Defaults

`interface Box<T = string>` supplies a default type argument.

code_examples.title: Generic API wrapper

title: Type inference
summary: TypeScript often figures out types from assigned values, return expressions, and usage context, reducing unnecessary annotations.
content:
## How inference works

If you write `const count = 1`, TypeScript knows `count` is a number. Function returns, array items, object properties, and callback parameters can also be inferred from context.

## When to annotate

Add explicit types at API boundaries, exported functions, complex generics, and places where inference becomes too wide.

## Common pitfall

Empty arrays and objects may need context. `const items = []` can become too loose unless you provide a type.

code_examples.title: Inference with context

related_questions.preview: What is type inference in TypeScript?

title: strict mode and compiler safety
summary: `strict` enables the safest default checks, including strict null handling and no implicit any.
content:
## Why strict matters

`strict: true` turns TypeScript from light autocomplete into a serious safety net. It catches unsafe null access, implicit `any`, weak function typing, and several edge cases around class fields and bind/call/apply.

## Production recommendation

For new projects, start strict from day one. For migrations, enable strict options gradually and track the remaining unsafe areas.

## Interview line

Strict mode catches the bugs JavaScript would let through silently.

code_examples.title: tsconfig strict mode

title: any vs unknown
summary: `any` disables checking; `unknown` accepts any value but forces narrowing before use.
content:
## any

`any` is an escape hatch. Once a value is `any`, TypeScript stops protecting you for that value and operations chained from it.

## unknown

`unknown` is safer for API responses, parsed JSON, message events, and user input. You must prove what it is before using it.

## Rule of thumb

Use `unknown` at trust boundaries and narrow it with checks, type guards, or runtime schemas.

code_examples.title: Narrow unknown before use

title: Union and intersection types
summary: Unions model alternatives; intersections combine requirements into one type.
content:
## Union types

`A | B` means the value can be A or B. You usually narrow before using properties that only exist on one branch.

## Intersection types

`A & B` means the value must satisfy both A and B. This is useful for composing object capabilities.

## Practical use

Use unions for status, API variants, and UI states. Use intersections to combine base props with feature-specific props.

code_examples.title: Union and intersection

title: Literal types and as const
summary: Literal types preserve exact values like `"admin"`; `as const` keeps object and array literals narrow and readonly.
content:
## Literal types

A literal type is an exact value type, such as `"admin"`, `200`, or `true`. They are powerful for statuses, roles, variants, and event names.

## as const

`as const` tells TypeScript not to widen values. Arrays become readonly tuples and object properties become readonly exact values.

## Common pattern

Define runtime arrays with `as const`, then derive a union type from them.

code_examples.title: Derive union from constants

title: Type narrowing and type guards
summary: Narrowing refines a broad type into a safer specific type using checks like `typeof`, `in`, `instanceof`, and custom guards.
content:
## Built-in narrowing

TypeScript understands common runtime checks: `typeof`, `instanceof`, truthiness, equality checks, and `in` checks.

## Custom type guards

A function returning `value is SomeType` tells TypeScript that a runtime check proves a more specific type.

## Why it matters

Narrowing is the bridge between unsafe external data and safe typed code.

code_examples.title: Custom type guard

title: Discriminated unions
summary: A discriminated union uses a shared tag field to model safe alternatives and make branching exhaustive.
content:
## Shape

Each variant has a common field, often `type`, `kind`, or `status`, with a different literal value.

## Why it is useful

This is one of TypeScript's best patterns for UI state, API responses, reducers, workflow nodes, and events.

## Exhaustiveness

Pair with `never` in the default branch to catch missing cases when a new variant is added.

code_examples.title: Safe UI state

title: never and exhaustiveness checks
summary: `never` represents impossible values and helps catch missing branches in switches and reducers.
content:
## What never means

`never` is the type of a value that should not exist. A function that always throws or never returns can return `never`.

## Exhaustive switches

When all union branches are handled, the leftover type becomes `never`. Assigning the default case to `never` catches future missing cases.

## Production use

Use it in reducers, API response handlers, and workflow engines where missing a new variant could become a bug.

code_examples.title: Exhaustive switch

title: keyof, typeof, and indexed access
summary: `keyof`, `typeof`, and indexed access types let you derive types from existing objects and keep code synchronized.
content:
## keyof

`keyof T` creates a union of the property names of T.

## typeof

In type positions, `typeof value` extracts the static type of a runtime value.

## Indexed access

`T[K]` gets the type of a property. Combined together, these tools prevent duplicate hand-written types.

code_examples.title: Derive types from a config object

title: Utility types
summary: Utility types transform existing types so you do not duplicate object shapes by hand.
content:
## Common utilities

`Partial<T>` makes properties optional. `Required<T>` makes them required. `Pick<T, K>` selects fields. `Omit<T, K>` removes fields. `Record<K, V>` creates an object type with known keys and values.

## Why they matter

They keep API payloads, form drafts, update requests, and view models tied to the source type.

## Avoid overuse

If a transformed type becomes hard to read, name it clearly.

code_examples.title: Update payload

title: Mapped types
summary: Mapped types loop over keys in type space to create new object types from existing ones.
content:
## Core idea

A mapped type uses `[K in keyof T]` to transform every property in a type.

## Common transformations

Make fields readonly, optional, nullable, required, or convert property values to another wrapper type.

## Real use

Form error objects, feature flag maps, DTO transformations, and permission maps often use mapped types.

code_examples.title: Map fields to errors

title: Conditional types and infer
summary: Conditional types choose one type or another based on assignability; `infer` extracts a type from another type.
content:
## Conditional types

`T extends U ? X : Y` means: if T is assignable to U, use X, otherwise use Y.

## infer

`infer` introduces a temporary type variable inside a conditional type. It is commonly used to extract promise results, function returns, array items, and component props.

## Keep it readable

Advanced types are powerful, but teams should name them well and avoid puzzle-code type systems.

code_examples.title: Extract Promise value

title: Template literal types
summary: Template literal types build string unions from other string types, useful for event names, route names, and CSS-like tokens.
content:
## Pattern

Template literal types use the same `${...}` idea as JavaScript template strings, but in type space.

## Common uses

Typed event names, route strings, analytics keys, API resource names, and design-system tokens.

## Practical advice

Use them when they remove real duplication. Avoid making every string in an app into a giant type puzzle.

code_examples.title: Typed event names

title: satisfies operator
summary: `satisfies` checks that a value matches a type without widening away its useful literal information.
content:
## Why it exists

A type annotation can widen precise values. `satisfies` verifies the object shape while preserving the exact inferred type of the value.

## Best use cases

Configuration objects, route maps, design tokens, feature flag definitions, and lookup tables.

## Interview line

`satisfies` is great when you want validation against a contract without losing precise inference.

code_examples.title: Config stays precise

title: Function types and overloads
summary: Function types define parameter and return contracts; overloads describe multiple valid call signatures.
content:
## Function types

Use function types for callbacks, handlers, async utilities, and reusable APIs.

## Overloads

Overloads are useful when one function supports different input shapes and returns different output shapes.

## Prefer simple unions first

If a union parameter is clear enough, use that. Reach for overloads when return type depends on input type.

code_examples.title: Overload return by input

title: Tuples and readonly arrays
summary: Tuples type fixed-position arrays; readonly arrays prevent mutation through the typed API.
content:
## Tuples

Use tuples when positions matter, such as `[state, setState]`, coordinate pairs, or result pairs.

## readonly arrays

`readonly T[]` or `ReadonlyArray<T>` prevents pushing, splicing, or replacing items through that reference.

## Practical use

Readonly inputs make utilities safer because callers know their arrays will not be mutated.

code_examples.title: Tuple and readonly input

title: Classes and access modifiers
summary: TypeScript adds compile-time class checks: `public`, `private`, `protected`, `readonly`, abstract classes, and implements contracts.
content:
## Access modifiers

`public` is default. `private` restricts access to the class. `protected` allows access in subclasses. `readonly` prevents reassignment after construction.

## Abstract classes

Abstract classes define shared implementation plus required methods for subclasses.

## Runtime note

TypeScript's `private` is mostly compile-time. JavaScript `#private` fields are runtime-private.

code_examples.title: Class contract

title: Enums vs union literals
summary: Enums create runtime objects; string literal unions are often smaller and easier to tree-shake.
content:
## Enums

Enums group named constants, but normal enums emit JavaScript. That can matter for bundle size and runtime behavior.

## Union literals

A union like `"admin" | "editor" | "viewer"` is erased at compile time and works well with `as const` arrays.

## Team choice

Many frontend teams prefer union literals for statuses and variants, and reserve enums for interoperability with existing APIs or codebases.

code_examples.title: Union alternative to enum

title: Modules and declaration files
summary: TypeScript uses ES modules for imports/exports and `.d.ts` files to describe types for JavaScript or compiled packages.
content:
## Modules

Use `export` and `import` to share values and types between files. Type-only imports make intent clear and can reduce emitted code confusion.

## Declaration files

`.d.ts` files contain type declarations without runtime implementation. They describe libraries, globals, CSS modules, image imports, or compiled packages.

## Common use

When a JavaScript package has no bundled types, teams may install `@types/package` or write a small local declaration file.

code_examples.title: Type-only import and declaration

title: tsconfig paths and build options
summary: `tsconfig.json` controls strictness, module output, path aliases, included files, and project build behavior.
content:
## Important options

`target` controls emitted JavaScript syntax. `module` controls module format. `strict` controls safety checks. `paths` defines aliases. `noEmit` type-checks without writing files.

## App vs library

Apps often let a bundler emit JavaScript and run `tsc --noEmit` for type checking. Libraries often use `declaration: true` to emit `.d.ts` files.

## Monorepos

Project references and `composite` help large TypeScript workspaces build incrementally.

code_examples.title: Common app config sketch

title: Typing React props and hooks
summary: TypeScript makes React components safer by typing props, events, state, refs, and custom hooks.
content:
## Props

Type component props directly. Prefer clear named prop types for exported components.

## Hooks

`useState` often infers well, but add a generic when initial state is `null`, an empty array, or a union. Type refs and events when inference is not enough.

## Custom hooks

Return named objects for clarity, or tuples with `as const` when tuple positions matter.

code_examples.title: Typed component and hook

related_questions.preview: How do you type React props?

title: Runtime validation with Zod
summary: TypeScript types disappear at runtime, so external data still needs validation with a schema library such as Zod.
content:
## Compile time vs runtime

TypeScript checks code before it runs. It cannot guarantee that an API, localStorage value, form input, or LLM response actually matches your type.

## Zod pattern

Define a schema, parse untrusted data, and infer the TypeScript type from the schema. That keeps runtime validation and compile-time types in sync.

## Interview line

Use TypeScript for developer safety and Zod for trust boundaries.

code_examples.title: Schema-first API parsing

related_questions.preview: How do you validate LLM JSON output?
